name: Discrete Bayesian Belief-Utility Model with Affective Priors
description: Probabilistic decision-theoretic framework where the agent maintains Bayesian beliefs about food locations and computes expected utility of each action under opponent-process-shaped utility functions that shift with allostatic load. Affective dynamics (hedonic state h, opponent state o) update in discrete time. A dysphoria index D captures reward deficit relative to the drifting allostatic set-point theta. Actions are selected via softmax over expected utilities, where utility is the product of dysphoria-weighted expected food reward plus an exploration bonus minus movement cost. As allostatic load accumulates, the dysphoria term dominates and the agent becomes compulsively food-focused, modelling narrowing of motivational repertoire (Koob & Le Moal, 2008 P4).

variables.name: hedonic_state
variables.description: Latent hedonic affective valence — the a-process analogue; updated by food reward

variables.name: opponent_state
variables.description: Latent aversive accumulator — the b-process analogue; driven by hedonic state, decays slowly

variables.name: net_affect
variables.description: Net affective state: A = h - o

variables.name: allostatic_set_point
variables.description: Reward reference level; drifts downward with chronic opponent-process activity

variables.name: dysphoria_index
variables.description: Reward deficit driving negative-reinforcement motivation: D = max(0, theta - A)

variables.name: food_belief_map
variables.description: Map from (x,y) grid cell to probability of food being present at that cell

variables.name: observed_reward
variables.description: Binary reward from last step: 1 if food consumed, 0 otherwise

parameters.name: opponent_recruitment_gain
parameters.source: Solomon & Corbit (1974): coupling strength from a to b

parameters.name: allostatic_drift_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): rate of set-point degradation

parameters.name: set_point_recovery_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): slow recovery toward baseline

parameters.name: baseline_set_point
parameters.source: Normalised baseline per Koob & Le Moal (2001)

parameters.name: palatability_boost
parameters.source: Normalised stimulus intensity; overridden by food's palatability property

parameters.name: stress_load_increment
parameters.source: Yau & Potenza (2013): stress accelerates allostatic drift

parameters.name: food_seeking_utility_weight
parameters.source: Derived from P3-P4: eating has high negative-reinforcement utility

parameters.name: food_prior_probability
parameters.source: Environmental assumption: prior belief food exists in unseen cell

parameters.name: movement_action_cost
parameters.source: Small cost to penalise unnecessary movement

rules.description: Hedonic state update: decays toward zero, boosted by food consumption scaled by palatability
Opponent state update: decays slowly, recruited proportionally to current hedonic state via fixed gain gamma
Dysphoria index: how far below the set-point the agent's affect currently is
Allostatic set-point drift: theta degrades proportionally to opponent state, with slow recovery
rules.pseudocode: theta = theta - eta * o + rho * (theta_0 - theta)

rules.description: Bayesian food belief update: observed cells get probability 1 (food present) or 0 (empty); unobserved cells keep prior
Expected utility computation for each action, combining dysphoria-weighted food reward, exploration value, and movement cost
rules.pseudocode:
for action in available_actions:
    if action == 'eat':
        food_here = any food at (my_x, my_y)
        expected_reward = 1.0 if food_here else 0.0
        U[action] = w_food * D * expected_reward
    elif action in ['move_up','move_down','move_left','move_right']:
        target_x, target_y = compute_target(action)
        # Expected reward = food probability at target, discounted further by distance to nearest food beyond
        expected_reward = f_beliefs.get((target_x, target_y), p_prior)
        # Also consider how move gets us closer to known food
        if known_food_positions:
            nearest_food = min(known_food_positions, key=manhattan_dist_from_target)
            dist = manhattan_distance(target, nearest_food)
            distance_discount = 1.0 / (1.0 + dist)
            expected_reward = max(expected_reward, distance_discount)
        info_gain = count_new_cells_revealed(target_x, target_y)
        U[action] = w_food * D * expected_reward + w_explore * info_gain - move_cost
    elif action == 'stay':
        U[action] = 0.0

rules.description: Softmax action selection: probability of each action proportional to exp(U / tau)
rules.pseudocode:
for each action a:
    prob[a] = exp(U[a] / tau)
prob = normalise(prob)
chosen_action = sample_from(prob)

decision_logic.description: Probabilistic (softmax) action selection over expected utilities. The expected utility of eating is proportional to dysphoria D times the certainty of food at current position. Moving toward food is weighted by D times the probability of food at/near the destination. Exploration contributes via information gain. As allostatic load accumulates and D grows, food-seeking utility dominates, modelling compulsive narrowing of the behavioural repertoire.
pseudocode: visible_food_positions = {(f['x'], f['y']): f for f in perception['resources']['food']}
# Mark all visible cells; cells with food get prob=1, empty visible cells get prob=0
# Unobserved cells retain their prior or last-known belief
# 2. Update internal affective state using reward from last step
phi_actual = consumed_food_palatability if r == 1 else phi
theta = theta - eta * o + rho * (theta_0 - theta)
# 4. Compute expected utility for each action
U['eat'] = w_food * D * (1.0 if food_here else 0.0)
# Also consider proximity to nearest known food
nearest_dist = min(abs(fx - tx) + abs(fy - ty) for fx, fy in visible_food_positions)
info_gain = 1.0 if (tx, ty) not in f_beliefs else 0.0
U[direction] = w_food * D * expected_food + w_explore * info_gain - move_cost
exp_u = {a: exp((u - max_u) / tau) for a, u in U.items()}
probs = {a: v / total for a, v in exp_u.items()}

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception['resources']['food']  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.palatability: food['palatability'] for consumed food item — used as phi_actual in R1
perception_to_variables.reward_signal: reward parameter from update(action, reward, new_perception): 1.0 if ate food, else 0.0 → maps to r

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect with reward 1.0; food palatability property [0.1, 1.0] used as phi_actual multiplier

expected_behaviors.description: Probabilistic food-seeking intensifies with allostatic load: as D grows, the softmax distribution concentrates on food-approaching actions
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: track entropy of action distribution over 200 steps → assert entropy decreases as theta drifts down

expected_behaviors.description: Dysphoria-driven eating: after chronic exposure, eating occurs primarily when D > 0 (negative reinforcement), not when h is high (positive reinforcement)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: in last 50 eat events, assert D > 0.5 in majority

expected_behaviors.description: Exploration suppression under high allostatic load: when D is large, exploration component becomes negligible relative to food-seeking
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compute ratio of w_food*D to w_explore over time → assert ratio grows as theta drops

expected_behaviors.description: Allostatic set-point drift: theta decreases over repeated eating episodes
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 200 steps with food available → assert theta at step 200 < theta at step 0

expected_behaviors.description: Withdrawal affect during abstinence: removing food after chronic exposure causes D to remain elevated and A to be negative
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: feed agent for 100 steps, remove food for 50 steps → assert average A < 0 during abstinence

references: Solomon, R.L. & Corbit, J.D. (1974). An opponent-process theory of motivation: I. Temporal dynamics of affect. Psychological Review, 81(2), 119–145.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2001). Drug addiction, dysregulation of reward, and allostasis. Neuropsychopharmacology, 24(2), 97–129.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2008). Neurobiological mechanisms for opponent motivational processes in addiction. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 363(1507), 3113–3123.
Yau, Y.H.C. & Potenza, M.N. (2013). Stress and eating behaviors. Minerva Endocrinologica, 38(3), 255–267.
